1.1: Establish and Maintain Detailed Enterprise Asset Inventory Organizations must establish and maintain an accurate, comprehensive, and regularly updated inventory of all enterprise assets capable of storing or processing data. This includes: End-user devices (e.g., laptops, desktops, mobile devices) Network devices (e.g., routers, switches, firewalls) Non-computing/IoT devices (e.g., printers, cameras, sensors) Physical and virtual servers, including cloud-based systems For each asset, the inventory should record: Network address (if static) Hardware (MAC) address Machine name Asset owner Department or business unit Approval status for network access
1.2: Address Unauthorized Assets Organizations must ensure there is an established process to detect and respond to unauthorized assets on a weekly basis or more frequently, depending on risk tolerance and operational needs. When an asset is detected that is not listed in the authorized enterprise asset inventory (see Safeguard 1.1), it must be addressed through one or more actions: Remove the asset from the network Deny the asset remote access Quarantine the asset until reviewed and authorized This process should be automated where possible, using network monitoring tools to scan for unrecognized devices and flag them for response. Even if an unauthorized asset is no longer visible on the network, it should be logged as resolved or unreachable, based on the assumption that it may have been removed.
1.3: Utilize an Active Discovery Tool Organizations should deploy active asset discovery tools to identify all devices connected to the enterprise network. Active discovery tools initiate network communication (e.g., ping sweeps, port scans, SNMP queries) to detect devices and gather details such as IP address, host name, and open ports. These tools should be configured to scan the environment at least once per day, or more frequently in dynamic or high-risk environments. The goal is to quickly detect new or unauthorized devices and compare them against the known enterprise asset inventory- This safeguard helps ensure that new, rogue, or misconfigured devices are not silently added to the environment without detection. It complements passive monitoring by actively probing the environment for visibility gaps.
1.4: Use Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) Logging to Update Enterprise Asset Inventory Organizations should configure Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) logging on all DHCP servers and/or IP address management (IPAM) tools to maintain an accurate and current asset inventory. These logs can provide valuable data on devices connecting to the network, including: IP address leases MAC addresses Hostnames Lease timestamps The logs should be reviewed and reconciled with the enterprise’s asset inventory at least weekly, or more frequently in larger or more dynamic environments. DHCP logs serve as a passive discovery mechanism and help detect transient devices that may connect briefly or irregularly. This safeguard helps fill visibility gaps left by active discovery tools and provides a lightweight method to track asset presence and changes.
1.5: Use a Passive Asset Discovery Tool Organizations should implement and maintain passive asset discovery tools to identify devices connected to the enterprise network without actively scanning. These tools monitor network traffic and extract metadata such as IP addresses, MAC addresses, protocols, and device types to detect connected assets in real time. Unlike active discovery tools (which send probes), passive tools operate by listening to traffic, making them suitable for environments where active scanning may disrupt operations or miss intermittently connected devices. Scan results from passive tools should be reviewed and used to update the enterprise asset inventory at least weekly, or more frequently where possible. This safeguard enhances asset visibility by continuously identifying devices that may be missed by active scanning or not logged through DHCP, ensuring more complete coverage of network-connected assets.
2.1: Establish and Maintain a Software Inventory Organizations must maintain a complete inventory of all licensed software installed on enterprise assets. This includes: Title, publisher, install date, business purpose Version, URL, deployment mechanism, decommission date A detailed and updated software inventory provides essential visibility for IT and security teams. It enables better patch management, compliance tracking, and identification of unauthorized or outdated applications. With clear documentation, the organization can more easily identify outdated or unsupported software and take proactive steps to mitigate associated risks. This safeguard provides the foundation for all other software control activities by ensuring visibility into what is running across the environment
2.2: Ensure Authorized Software is Currently Supported Ensure that only currently supported software is designated as authorized in the software inventory for enterprise assets. If software is unsupported yet necessary for the fulfillment of the enterprise's mission, document an exception detailing mitigating controls and residual risk acceptance. For any unsupported software without an exception documentation, designate as unauthorized. Review the software list to verify software support at least monthly, or more frequently.
2.3: Address Unauthorized Software Enterprises must detect and address software that is not listed in the authorized software inventory. This includes removing or documenting exceptions for unauthorized software. Scanning and resolution should occur monthly or more frequently. This safeguard limits shadow IT and reduces the attack surface introduced by unvetted applications.
2.4: Utilize Automated Software Inventory Tools Deploy enterprise-wide software inventory tools to automate the discovery and documentation of installed software. This safeguard increases accuracy and efficiency in software tracking by leveraging automation.
2.5: Allowlist Authorized Software Use technical controls to ensure that only authorized software can run. Controls can include allowlisting tools, execution restrictions, or OS-level policies. This safeguard provides a strong defense against malware and unauthorized software execution.
2.6: Allowlist Authorized Libraries Use technical controls to ensure that only authorized software libraries, such as specific .dll, .ocx, .so, etc., files, are allowed to load into a system process. Block unauthorized libraries from loading into a system process. Reassess bi-annually, or more frequently. This safeguard protects processes from being hijacked through malicious or outdated shared libraries.
2.7: Allowlist Authorized Scripts Use technical controls, such as digital signatures and version control, to ensure that only authorized scripts, such as specific .ps1, .py, etc., files, are allowed to execute. Block unauthorized scripts from executing. Reassess bi-annually, or more frequently. This safeguard protects against script-based attacks and ensures that automation follows approved standards.
3.1: Establish and Maintain a Data Management Process Establish and maintain a data management process. In the process, address data sensitivity, data owner, handling of data, data retention limits, and disposal requirements, based on sensitivity and retention standards for the enterprise. Review and update documentation annually, or when significant enterprise changes occur that could impact this Safeguard. This safeguard ensures that sensitive data is governed effectively by organizational policies tailored to its sensitivity.
3.2: Establish and Maintain a Data Inventory A current inventory of sensitive data helps track what information exists, where it resides, and who can access it. This inventory forms the basis for applying appropriate controls and ensuring compliance with privacy laws This safeguard supports data visibility and governance by ensuring sensitive data is mapped to systems and ownership.
3.3: Configure Data Access Control Lists Access to sensitive data should be restricted based on need-to-know principles. Access Control Lists (ACLs) must be defined and implemented to control who can access or modify data. This safeguard reduces the risk of data exposure by limiting access to authorized personnel only.
3.4: Enforce Data Retention Retain data according to the enterprise’s data management process. Data retention must include both minimum and maximum timelines. This safeguard minimizes risk and storage overhead by defining and enforcing data lifecycle boundaries
3.5: Securely Dispose of Data Securely dispose of data as outlined in the enterprise’s data management process. Ensure the disposal process and method are commensurate with the data sensitivity. This safeguard reduces the risk of data leaks from retired or lost storage devices.
3.6: Encrypt Data on End-User Devices Encrypt data on end-user devices containing sensitive data. Example implementations can include, Windows BitLocker®, Apple FileVault®, Linux® dm-crypt. This safeguard protects data in case of device theft or loss.
3.7: Establish and Maintain a Data Classification Scheme Define data classification levels (e.g., Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) and ensure that all data is labeled accordingly. This supports consistent handling across the enterprise. This safeguard enhances policy enforcement by making data sensitivity explicit.
3.8: Document Data Flows Map and document how data moves within and outside the enterprise, including to cloud providers and partners. This helps in assessing data exposure. This safeguard provides visibility into how data is transmitted and stored across systems.
3.9: Encrypt Data on Removable Media Sensitive data stored on removable drives (e.g., USB sticks, SD cards) must be encrypted to prevent unauthorized access in case of loss. This safeguard ensures that portable data remains protected when outside enterprise control.
3.10: Encrypt Sensitive Data in Transit Use secure protocols like TLS, SSH, or VPNs to protect sensitive data moving over networks. This safeguard mitigates eavesdropping or interception risks during data transmission.
3.11: Encrypt Sensitive Data At Rest Encrypt sensitive data at rest on servers, applications, and databases containing sensitive data. Storage-layer encryption, also known as server-side encryption, meets the minimum requirement of this Safeguard. Additional encryption methods may include application-layer encryption, also known as client-side encryption, where access to the data storage device(s) does not permit access to the plain-text data. This safeguard limits access to data in case of storage compromise or database breaches.
3.12: Segment Data Processing and Storage Based on Sensitivity Segment data processing and storage based on the sensitivity of the data. Do not process sensitive data on enterprise assets intended for lower sensitivity data. This safeguard enforces defense-in-depth by preventing overexposure of high-risk data.
3.13: Deploy a Data Loss Prevention Solution Use automated tools like DLP to monitor, detect, and block unauthorized data movement. This safeguard prevents data exfiltration and ensures adherence to data handling rules.
3.14: Log Sensitive Data Access Record access to sensitive data, including reading, editing, and deletion, to support auditing and incident investigation. This safeguard provides accountability and forensic capability in the event of a breach.
4.1: Establish and Maintain a Secure Configuration Process Define a process for implementing secure configurations across enterprise systems. This process should include creating and maintaining configuration standards based on recognized benchmarks (e.g., CIS, DISA STIGs), ensuring all systems are deployed according to these standards, and documenting approved exceptions. This safeguard lays the foundation for system hardening by ensuring a consistent and secure baseline is enforced across all assets.
4.2: Establish and Maintain a Secure Configuration Process for Network Infrastructure Extend the configuration process to routers, switches, firewalls, and other network infrastructure. Document, review, and update configuration guidelines regularly to ensure compliance with secure architecture principles. This safeguard enforces strong configurations on critical network components to prevent exploitation.
4.3: Configure Automatic Session Locking on Enterprise Assets Configure devices to automatically lock sessions after a defined inactivity period to prevent unauthorized access. This safeguard reduces the risk of unauthorized access through unattended devices.
4.4: Implement and Manage a Firewall on Servers Deploy host-based or OS-level firewalls on all servers. Only allow essential traffic. This safeguard restricts unnecessary communication to and from servers, reducing attack surface.
4.5: Implement and Manage a Firewall on End-User Devices Install and manage host-based firewalls or filtering tools with a default deny rule on all endpoints. This safeguard helps block malicious connections and reduces endpoint vulnerability.
4.6: Securely Manage Enterprise Assets and Software Ensure administrative interfaces and configuration tools are accessed over secure channels (e.g., SSH, HTTPS), and avoid insecure protocols. This safeguard prevents interception and tampering with management traffic.
4.7: Manage Default Accounts on Enterprise Assets and Software Disable or secure all default vendor accounts. If disabling is not feasible, change credentials and restrict access. This safeguard mitigates the risk of attackers exploiting well-known default credentials.
4.8: Uninstall or Disable Unnecessary Services on Enterprise Assets and Software Remove unused services and features that are not essential to business operations to reduce the potential attack surface. This safeguard reduces the likelihood of exploitation by minimizing unnecessary functionalities.
4.9: Configure Trusted DNS Servers on Enterprise Assets Configure devices to use enterprise-approved or reputable DNS servers to prevent DNS hijacking and filtering issues. Implementation Groups: IG2, IG3 This safeguard helps enforce secure and reliable domain name resolution.
4.10: Enforce Automatic Device Lockout on Portable End-User Devices Enable lockout mechanisms after failed login attempts to prevent brute force attacks. This safeguard helps prevent unauthorized access in case of lost or stolen devices.
4.11: Enforce Remote Wipe Capability on Portable End-User Devices Enable remote wipe capabilities for enterprise-owned mobile devices to remove data if lost or stolen. This safeguard enables rapid response to data loss threats from mobile devices.
4.12: Separate Enterprise Workspaces on Mobile End-User Devices Separate personal and enterprise data on mobile devices using MDM solutions to ensure business data is handled securely. This safeguard protects enterprise data on mobile devices and ensures compliance with privacy and security policies.
5.1: Establish and Maintain an Inventory of Accounts Maintain a detailed inventory of all accounts, including users and administrators. Ensure each entry includes owner details, purpose, and lifecycle status. This safeguard ensures visibility and control over who has access to enterprise systems and data.
5.2: Use Unique Passwords All accounts must use unique passwords that meet enterprise security policies, with stronger requirements for those not using MFA. This safeguard reduces the risk of credential stuffing and lateral movement by attackers.
5.3: Disable Dormant Accounts Accounts that have not been used within 45 days should be automatically disabled unless explicitly approved. This safeguard helps reduce attack surfaces by eliminating forgotten or unused accounts.
5.4: Restrict Administrator Privileges to Dedicated Administrator Accounts Administrators must use separate, non-privileged accounts for everyday activities such as browsing or email. This safeguard limits exposure of privileged accounts to phishing and malware threats.
5.5: Establish and Maintain an Inventory of Service Accounts Maintain a registry of service accounts, including their ownership, use, and review history. Review periodically to validate legitimacy. This safeguard brings transparency and governance to non-human access points in enterprise systems.
5.6: Centralize Account Management Use centralized identity services (e.g., Active Directory, Azure AD) to manage account provisioning, authentication, and access control. This safeguard simplifies administration, enhances visibility, and supports consistent enforcement of policies.
6.1: Establish an Access Granting Process Define and document a formal process for granting access upon hiring, change in role, or new permissions. Automate where possible. This safeguard ensures that access rights are appropriately aligned with job roles from the start.
6.2: Establish an Access Revoking Process Implement a process to revoke access upon user departure, role change, or privilege revocation. This includes account disabling to preserve audit trails. This safeguard limits continued access by former employees or accounts no longer in use.
6.3: Require MFA for Externally-Exposed Applications All third-party or externally-facing applications must enforce MFA for user authentication. This safeguard significantly reduces the risk of unauthorized access through compromised credentials.
6.4: Require MFA for Remote Network Access All remote access (e.g., VPN, RDP) must require multi-factor authentication. This safeguard protects against unauthorized access over remote connections.
6.5: Require MFA for Administrative Access Use MFA for all administrative accounts on enterprise assets. This safeguard strengthens protection of privileged accounts, which are high-value targets.
6.6: Establish and Maintain an Inventory of Authentication and Authorization Systems Document all systems used for managing authentication and authorization, and update the inventory at least annually. This safeguard ensures visibility and oversight of systems that govern access control.
6.7: Centralize Access Control Consolidate identity and access management via a central directory service or single sign-on (SSO) provider. This safeguard enhances control, consistency, and auditability of access.
6.8: Define and Maintain Role-Based Access Control Establish roles and assign access based on those roles. Regularly review access rights to ensure alignment with current responsibilities. This safeguard enforces least-privilege access and simplifies permission audits.
7.1: Establish and Maintain a Vulnerability Management Process Document a vulnerability management process for identifying and handling software weaknesses. Update the documentation annually or after significant changes. This safeguard ensures a structured and timely approach to identifying and addressing system vulnerabilities.
7.2: Establish and Maintain a Remediation Process Define a risk-based remediation process that prioritizes critical and sensitive systems. Review the strategy monthly or more frequently. This safeguard enables the organization to act efficiently on high-risk vulnerabilities.
7.3: Perform Automated Operating System Patch Management Deploy OS patches via automated tools at least monthly. Compare running versions with an approved baseline. This safeguard minimizes exposure to OS-level vulnerabilities.
7.4: Perform Automated Application Patch Management Automatically patch third-party and internal applications monthly or more frequently. This safeguard ensures application-level vulnerabilities are addressed consistently and quickly.
7.5: Perform Automated Vulnerability Scans of Internal Enterprise Assets Run quarterly authenticated and unauthenticated scans on internal systems. Use SCAP-compliant tools. This safeguard enhances awareness of vulnerabilities across all internal infrastructure.
7.6: Perform Automated Vulnerability Scans of Externally-Exposed Enterprise Assets Perform monthly scans on public-facing systems and cloud resources. Use industry-standard tools. This safeguard helps detect and close external-facing security gaps.
7.7: Remediate Detected Vulnerabilities Actively remediate identified vulnerabilities based on scan results and enterprise prioritization. This safeguard closes the loop by ensuring that identified risks are addressed and tracked.
8.1: Establish and Maintain an Audit Log Management Process Document an audit log management policy that defines logging requirements, including log collection, review, and retention. Review the documentation annually or after significant changes. This safeguard ensures the enterprise has a clear and structured approach to handling audit logs.
8.2: Collect Audit Logs Ensure that logging is enabled across all enterprise assets as per the audit log management process. This safeguard provides visibility into enterprise activities by ensuring logs are actively collected.
8.3: Ensure Adequate Audit Log Storage Verify that logging destinations have sufficient capacity to retain logs in line with retention policies. This safeguard ensures logs are not lost due to storage limitations and are retained for investigations.
8.4: Standardize Time Synchronization Configure enterprise assets with at least two synchronized time sources to ensure consistent log timestamps. This safeguard ensures accurate log correlation by enforcing time consistency across systems.
8.5: Collect Detailed Audit Logs Enable detailed logging on sensitive systems, including information such as event source, timestamp, and user identifiers. This safeguard enhances forensic analysis by providing rich detail in audit trails.
8.6: Collect DNS Query Audit Logs Enable audit logging on DNS servers to record DNS queries and support threat hunting. This safeguard enables detection of suspicious domain activity within the enterprise.
8.7: Collect URL Request Audit Logs Capture URL requests from web traffic where supported, especially on proxies or endpoint firewalls. This safeguard supports investigations and threat detection related to web activity.
8.8: Collect Command-Line Audit Logs Enable command-line logging to capture administrative actions on critical systems. This safeguard provides insight into administrative behavior and potential misuse of elevated privileges.
8.9: Centralize Audit Logs Aggregate logs into a centralized system for easier analysis, correlation, and long-term retention. This safeguard streamlines log management and facilitates real-time threat detection.
8.10: Retain Audit Logs Store audit logs for a minimum of 90 days to support security investigations and compliance This safeguard ensures log availability for delayed threat discovery and forensic review.
8.11: Conduct Audit Log Reviews Perform weekly reviews of audit logs to detect anomalies or suspicious activity. This safeguard ensures threats are discovered early through consistent log review practices.
8.12: Collect Service Provider Logs Collect logs from cloud and third-party services that include access events and user activity. This safeguard extends visibility into externally hosted environments to maintain accountability.
9.1: Ensure Use of Only Fully Supported Browsers and Email Clients Allow only fully supported browsers and email clients to be used in the enterprise, ensuring all software is up-to-date and vendor-supported. This safeguard reduces exposure to known vulnerabilities by restricting unsupported or outdated software.
9.2: Use DNS Filtering Services Deploy DNS filtering across enterprise assets to prevent access to malicious or unauthorized domains. This safeguard helps block threat access at the domain level before a connection is even made.
9.3: Maintain and Enforce Network-Based URL Filters Use network-level URL filters to prevent access to malicious or inappropriate websites using tools like proxies or firewalls. This safeguard limits user access to harmful websites, helping reduce the risk of social engineering and malware infections.
9.4: Restrict Unnecessary or Unauthorized Browser and Email Client Extensions Disable or remove unauthorized plugins and extensions in browsers and email clients to reduce attack surfaces. This safeguard prevents the introduction of insecure or malicious functionality through third-party add-ons.
9.5: Implement DMARC Deploy DMARC along with SPF and DKIM to validate the authenticity of email senders and block spoofed messages. This safeguard strengthens email authenticity and reduces successful phishing attempts.
9.6: Block Unnecessary File Types Configure email gateways to block file types commonly used in attacks (e.g., .exe, .js). This safeguard helps prevent malware delivery via email by filtering high-risk file types.
9.7: Deploy and Maintain Email Server Anti-Malware Protections Ensure email servers use anti-malware tools to scan incoming and outgoing messages. This safeguard detects and blocks malicious content in emails before it reaches users.
10.1: Deploy and Maintain Anti-Malware Software Install and maintain authorized anti-malware software on all supported enterprise assets. This safeguard ensures that enterprise devices are protected with vetted, actively maintained anti-malware solutions.
10.2: Configure Automatic Anti-Malware Signature Updates Ensure anti-malware software is configured to automatically receive and apply signature updates. This safeguard ensures that malware defenses stay effective against the latest threats.
10.3: Disable Autorun and Autoplay for Removable Media Disable auto-execution features for removable media to prevent automatic malware execution. This safeguard minimizes the risk of malware automatically launching from USB drives and other removable media.
10.4: Configure Automatic Anti-Malware Scanning of Removable Media Set anti-malware software to scan removable media automatically upon connection. This safeguard enhances detection of malicious content from external storage devices.
10.5: Enable Anti-Exploitation Features Enable operating system and application-level anti-exploitation capabilities. This safeguard provides additional layers of protection against sophisticated attacks that exploit vulnerabilities.
10.6: Centrally Manage Anti-Malware Software Use centralized management to control and monitor anti-malware software across the enterprise. This safeguard improves visibility and consistency in malware defense by centralizing control.
11.1: Establish and Maintain a Data Recovery Process Develop and maintain documented backup procedures that define recovery scope, data prioritization, and backup data security. This safeguard ensures backup activities are structured, well-documented, and aligned with business continuity goals.
11.2: Perform Automated Backups Set up automatic backup processes for in-scope systems and data. Run backups at least weekly, depending on data sensitivity. This safeguard minimizes data loss risk and supports rapid restoration in case of incident.
11.3: Protect Recovery Data Ensure backup data is protected at rest and in transit using encryption or data separation. This safeguard maintains the confidentiality and integrity of backup data.
11.4: Establish and Maintain an Isolated Instance of Recovery Data Maintain backup copies in isolated locations, either offline or in secure cloud environments. This safeguard protects recovery data from being compromised during an active attack.
11.5: Test Data Recovery Regularly test data recovery procedures by restoring a sample of backed-up data. This safeguard ensures that recovery processes work reliably and meet business needs in real-world scenarios.
12.1: Ensure Network Infrastructure is Up-to-Date Maintain the latest supported versions of network infrastructure devices and services to minimize exposure to known vulnerabilities. This safeguard ensures that critical network devices are not running outdated or unsupported software that could be exploited.
12.2: Establish and Maintain a Secure Network Architecture Design and enforce a secure network architecture that includes segmentation, least privilege, and availability measures. This safeguard enforces a defense-in-depth approach by minimizing exposure across different network zones.
12.3: Securely Manage Network Infrastructure Ensure network devices are configured securely using encrypted management protocols and infrastructure-as-code with version control. This safeguard promotes secure and auditable management practices for infrastructure.
12.4: Establish and Maintain Architecture Diagram(s) Create and regularly update network architecture documentation to reflect the current enterprise environment. This safeguard supports visibility, planning, and effective incident response through up-to-date network mapping.
12.5: Centralize Network Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (AAA) Use centralized systems to manage access to network infrastructure, consolidating redundant AAA services where possible. This safeguard helps ensure consistent and auditable access control for network management.
12.6: Use of Secure Network Management and Communication Protocols Enforce the use of secure protocols (e.g., HTTPS, SSH, WPA2 Enterprise) for managing and communicating across networks. This safeguard protects sensitive data in transit and reduces the risk of protocol-based attacks.
12.7: Ensure Remote Devices Utilize a VPN and are Connecting to an Enterprise's AAA Infrastructure Require remote users to authenticate through VPNs connected to enterprise AAA systems to securely access internal resources. This safeguard ensures remote access is secure, monitored, and consistent with enterprise access policies.
13.1: Centralize Security Event Alerting Implement centralized security event alerting, ideally through a SIEM or log analytics platform with correlation capabilities. This safeguard ensures centralized visibility and accelerates detection and response to threats.
13.2: Deploy a Host-Based Intrusion Detection Solution Use host-based intrusion detection systems (HIDS) to monitor activity and detect suspicious behavior on endpoints. This safeguard enhances endpoint threat detection and response capabilities.
13.3: Deploy a Network Intrusion Detection Solution Deploy a network intrusion detection system (NIDS) at strategic network boundaries. This safeguard improves visibility into external and internal threats traversing the network.
13.4: Perform Traffic Filtering Between Network Segments Filter traffic between network segments to restrict unauthorized access and lateral movement. This safeguard reduces attack surface and supports segmentation policies.
13.5: Manage Access Control for Remote Assets Ensure remote enterprise assets are governed by access control and policy enforcement mechanisms. This safeguard ensures remote endpoints comply with enterprise security standards before gaining access.
13.6: Collect Network Traffic Flow Logs Capture and analyze flow logs or traffic data from critical network infrastructure. This safeguard supports traffic monitoring and threat investigation.
13.7: Deploy a Host-Based Intrusion Prevention Solution Use host-based intrusion prevention tools like EDR to block malicious behavior. This safeguard blocks threats in real time at the endpoint level.
13.8: Deploy a Network Intrusion Prevention Solution Implement network-based intrusion prevention systems (NIPS) or equivalent cloud-native services. This safeguard blocks malicious traffic at the network perimeter and within cloud environments.
13.9: Deploy Port-Level Access Control Enforce port-level access control using 802.1x or similar protocols for device and user authentication. This safeguard enforces secure access at the network port level.
13.10: Perform Application Layer Filtering Filter traffic at the application layer using firewalls or proxies. This safeguard ensures fine-grained control and inspection of network traffic by application.
13.11: Tune Security Event Alerting Thresholds Regularly review and adjust alert thresholds to reduce false positives and improve detection. This safeguard optimizes alert quality, allowing the security team to focus on true threats.
14.1: Establish and Maintain a Security Awareness Program Develop and maintain a formal security awareness training program that is provided at hire and annually thereafter. The training must be reviewed and updated annually or after significant enterprise changes. This safeguard ensures foundational awareness across the organization, setting the tone for secure behavior.
14.2: Train Workforce Members to Recognize Social Engineering Attacks Educate employees on identifying and avoiding social engineering tactics such as phishing, pretexting, and tailgating. This safeguard reduces the likelihood of human error from social engineering tactics.
14.3: Train Workforce Members on Authentication Best Practices Deliver training on secure authentication, including password hygiene, multi-factor authentication, and credential management. This safeguard improves security posture by reinforcing secure identity management practices.
14.4: Train Workforce on Data Handling Best Practices Provide training on proper data handling procedures, including secure storage, transfer, disposal, and screen/desk privacy. This safeguard ensures sensitive data is properly managed throughout its lifecycle.
14.5: Train Workforce Members on Causes of Unintentional Data Exposure Educate the workforce on common causes of accidental data leakage, including misdelivery, device loss, and sharing with unauthorized audiences. This safeguard minimizes the risk of accidental data exposure due to human oversight.
14.6: Train Workforce Members on Recognizing and Reporting Security Incidents Ensure that workforce members can identify and appropriately report suspected security incidents. This safeguard accelerates incident detection and response through workforce engagement.
14.7: Train Workforce on How to Identify and Report if Their Enterprise Assets are Missing Security Updates Teach employees how to recognize if systems are not up to date and report the issue to IT. This safeguard supports proactive identification of vulnerabilities at the user level.
14.8: Train Workforce on the Dangers of Connecting to and Transmitting Enterprise Data Over Insecure Networks Train workforce members on the risks of using insecure networks, especially while remote, and how to secure their home environments. This safeguard helps prevent exposure of enterprise data over untrusted networks.
14.9: Conduct Role-Specific Security Awareness and Skills Training Deliver targeted training based on job roles, such as development, administration, or executive leadership. This safeguard ensures employees receive relevant and applicable security knowledge tailored to their responsibilities.
15.1: Establish and Maintain an Inventory of Service Providers Maintain a detailed inventory of all service providers, including their classification and assigned enterprise contacts. Update the list at least annually or when changes occur. This safeguard ensures transparency and accountability by tracking all active service providers.
15.2: Establish and Maintain a Service Provider Management Policy Develop and maintain a formal policy that governs how service providers are classified, assessed, monitored, and decommissioned. This safeguard ensures consistent governance of third-party risks.
15.3: Classify Service Providers Classify service providers based on characteristics such as data sensitivity, regulatory requirements, and risk. This safeguard helps prioritize monitoring and assessment efforts.
15.4: Ensure Service Provider Contracts Include Security Requirements Contracts with service providers must include appropriate security clauses aligned with enterprise policy. This safeguard ensures that legal agreements reflect security expectations.
15.5: Assess Service Providers Evaluate the security posture of service providers using SOC 2 reports, security questionnaires, or other validation mechanisms. This safeguard helps identify weaknesses and verify compliance with security requirements.
15.6: Monitor Service Providers Continuously monitor service providers for changes in risk posture or compliance gaps. This safeguard provides continuous visibility into the security status of external partners.
15.7: Securely Decommission Service Providers When terminating relationships, ensure secure data disposal, account deactivation, and documentation of the process. This safeguard ensures that data and access are properly secured during service provider offboarding.
16.1: Establish and Maintain a Secure Application Development Process Develop a comprehensive and formal secure application development process that includes design standards, secure coding practices, developer training, vulnerability management, third-party code security, and testing procedures. Review and update annually or after significant changes. This safeguard ensures a structured and proactive approach to secure software development.
16.2: Establish and Maintain a Process to Accept and Address Software Vulnerabilities Create and maintain a process for accepting vulnerability reports, including intake from external sources. The process must define roles, procedures for remediation, and severity tracking. This safeguard establishes a clear pathway to continuously address and remediate vulnerabilities.
16.3: Perform Root Cause Analysis on Security Vulnerabilities Conduct root cause analysis on security vulnerabilities to prevent recurrence. Use the insights to refine development practices and improve code quality. This safeguard fosters learning and continuous improvement in secure development.
16.4: Establish and Manage an Inventory of Third-Party Software Components Maintain an inventory (software bill of materials) of third-party components used in development. Evaluate monthly for risks, updates, and support status. This safeguard improves visibility into software supply chain risks.
16.5: Use Up-to-Date and Trusted Third-Party Software Components Ensure all third-party components are from trusted sources, up-to-date, and scanned for known vulnerabilities prior to use. This safeguard reduces exposure to vulnerabilities through third-party software.
16.6: Establish and Maintain a Severity Rating System and Process for Application Vulnerabilities Define and maintain a severity classification system to prioritize vulnerabilities and establish security release thresholds. This safeguard enables risk-based triage and effective remediation prioritization.
16.7: Use Standard Hardening Configuration Templates for Application Infrastructure Apply secure configuration standards to all supporting application infrastructure components including cloud services, databases, and servers. This safeguard ensures consistency and reduces misconfigurations across infrastructure.
16.8: Separate Production and Non-Production Systems Maintain distinct environments for development, testing, and production to prevent cross-contamination and enable safe testing. This safeguard improves environment integrity and limits accidental changes to live systems.
16.9: Train Developers in Application Security Concepts and Secure Coding Ensure that developers receive annual training on security topics relevant to their development environments and responsibilities. This safeguard reinforces secure coding behaviors and fosters a security-aware development culture.
16.10: Apply Secure Design Principles in Application Architectures Incorporate principles like least privilege, fail-safe defaults, and input validation into application architecture and infrastructure. This safeguard ensures applications are resilient against common design-level vulnerabilities.
16.11: Leverage Vetted Modules or Services for Application Security Components Use platform-vetted services and libraries for functions like authentication, encryption, and logging instead of custom code. This safeguard increases security through the use of reliable and thoroughly tested components.
16.12: Implement Code-Level Security Checks Apply automated static and dynamic analysis tools to ensure secure coding practices are followed throughout development. This safeguard helps detect flaws early in the development lifecycle before deployment.
16.13: Conduct Application Penetration Testing Conduct regular authenticated and unauthenticated penetration testing of applications, especially those deemed critical. This safeguard simulates real-world attacks to discover exploitable issues.
16.14: Conduct Threat Modeling Perform structured threat modeling early in the software design phase to identify and address potential vulnerabilities. This safeguard proactively identifies design flaws and guides security-focused architecture decisions.
17.1: Designate Personnel to Manage Incident Handling Purpose: Assign key personnel to lead and coordinate incident response activities. This ensures clear accountability and effective coordination in the event of a security incident. This safeguard ensures that the organization has designated leadership for handling incidents, providing structure and reducing confusion during critical response moments.
17.2: Establish and Maintain Contact Information for Reporting Security Incidents Purpose: Maintain up-to-date contact lists for all internal and external stakeholders who may need to be informed during a security incident. This safeguard ensures that the right parties can be reached quickly during a crisis, reducing delays in communication and incident resolution.
17.3: Establish and Maintain an Enterprise Process for Reporting Incidents Purpose: Define a clear and accessible process for all workforce members to report security incidents. This safeguard promotes early detection by enabling employees to quickly and confidently report potential security issues.
17.4: Establish and Maintain an Incident Response Process Purpose: Document and maintain a formal process for managing incidents, including roles, legal requirements, and communication protocols. This safeguard provides a structured approach to incident response, ensuring the organization acts swiftly, lawfully, and consistently.
17.5: Assign Key Roles and Responsibilities Purpose: Assign specific roles across departments—such as legal, IT, HR, and communications—to support coordinated incident response. This safeguard ensures that all critical functions are covered during an incident, avoiding gaps and miscommunication.
17.6: Define Mechanisms for Communicating During Incident Response Purpose: Identify backup and primary communication methods for use during incidents—especially if standard channels are compromised. This safeguard ensures continuity in communication, even when conventional tools may be inaccessible or compromised.
17.7: Conduct Routine Incident Response Exercises Purpose: Test the incident response plan through realistic exercises to ensure readiness and identify areas for improvement. This safeguard validates the effectiveness of the response plan and strengthens team preparedness through practice.
17.8: Conduct Post-Incident Reviews Purpose: After each incident, evaluate what occurred, what went well, and what could improve to prevent future occurrences. This safeguard transforms incidents into learning opportunities that strengthen future security posture.
17.9: Establish and Maintain Security Incident Thresholds Purpose: Clearly define what qualifies as an “incident” versus a lower-severity “event,” enabling consistent triage and prioritization. This safeguard provides clarity and consistency in handling security issues, enabling the organization to respond appropriately based on severity.
18.1: Establish and Maintain a Penetration Testing Program Purpose: Develop a structured penetration testing program that aligns with the enterprise’s size, complexity, and risk landscape. This includes defining scope, frequency, limitations, contact points, remediation workflows, and retrospective requirements. This safeguard ensures the organization has a formalized and repeatable approach to proactively identifying and managing security weaknesses.
18.2: Perform Periodic External Penetration Tests Purpose: Conduct regular external penetration tests—at minimum annually—to simulate real-world attack scenarios and uncover vulnerabilities in internet-facing assets. This safeguard ensures the organization regularly tests its external defenses against real-world attacker methods and maintains a hardened perimeter.
18.3: Remediate Penetration Test Findings Purpose: Address findings from penetration tests based on a clearly defined policy, ensuring that vulnerabilities are prioritized and remediated according to risk. This safeguard ensures that penetration test results lead to tangible improvements in security rather than being filed away without action.
18.4: Validate Security Measures Purpose: After each penetration test, evaluate and, if needed, adjust security tools and rulesets to detect or block the techniques that were tested. This safeguard ensures the organization continuously improves its detection and defense mechanisms based on lessons learned from testing.
18.5: Perform Periodic Internal Penetration Tests Purpose: Conduct internal penetration tests at least annually to uncover vulnerabilities that an attacker could exploit after gaining initial access. This safeguard ensures internal controls are tested regularly to detect weaknesses beyond the network perimete
